articles.headline: Human Rights Day 2025: India Reaffirms Commitments Amidst Evolving Challenges
articles.explainer:
• Celebrated globally on December 10th, marking the adoption of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) in 1948.
• India commemorates Human Rights Day by reflecting on its constitutional guarantees and international obligations.
• Focus this year includes digital rights, climate justice, and protection of vulnerable groups in a rapidly changing world.
• Discussions center on balancing national security with individual liberties, particularly in the context of emerging technologies.
• NHRC and civil society organizations highlight areas for improvement in human rights protection and awareness.
gsSubTopics: Human Rights
Constitutional provisions
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population
prelimsPoints: Human Rights Day is observed on December 10th.
The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) was adopted by the UN General Assembly on December 10, 1948.
India is a signatory to various international human rights covenants.
The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) is a statutory body in India.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's progress and challenges in upholding human rights since the adoption of the UDHR.
Discuss the role of the NHRC and civil society in promoting and protecting human rights in India.
Examine the impact of technological advancements (e.g., AI, surveillance) on human rights and the need for new legal frameworks.
Evaluate the intersection of climate change and human rights, particularly for marginalized communities in India.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic symbolizing human rights, possibly featuring the UDHR scroll and diverse hands reaching out.
articles.trivia: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights holds the Guinness World Record as the most translated document in the world, available in over 500 languages.

articles.headline: Parliament Winter Session: Key Amendments to Digital Personal Data Protection Act Debated
articles.explainer:
• The Winter Session of Parliament sees intense debate over proposed amendments to the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.
• Amendments aim to address concerns raised by industry stakeholders and privacy advocates regarding data localization and consent mechanisms.
• Focus on striking a balance between data innovation, national security, and individual privacy rights.
• Opposition parties demand clearer definitions for 'significant data fiduciaries' and the scope of government exemptions.
• The Joint Parliamentary Committee report on the proposed changes is expected to be tabled soon.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Information Technology
prelimsPoints: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act was enacted in 2023.
The Act aims to regulate the processing of digital personal data.
It defines 'Data Fiduciary' and 'Data Principal'.
Parliamentary sessions include Monsoon, Winter, and Budget sessions.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, and the necessity for its amendments.
Discuss the challenges in implementing a robust data protection framework in India, considering technological advancements and economic interests.
Analyze the implications of data localization mandates on global businesses and India's digital economy aspirations.
Examine the balance between national security concerns and individual privacy rights in the context of digital data.
articles.imageDescription: An illustration of the Indian Parliament building with digital data streams flowing around it.
articles.trivia: India's first attempt at a comprehensive data protection law began with the Justice B.N. Srikrishna Committee report in 2018.

articles.headline: RBI Holds Repo Rate Steady Amidst Easing Inflation, Cautions on Global Volatility
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) maintained the repo rate at its current level for the sixth consecutive review.
• Decision driven by a cautious approach, despite a marginal easing of retail inflation (CPI) in recent months.
• RBI Governor highlighted global economic uncertainties, geopolitical tensions, and potential impact on commodity prices.
• Forecasts for India's GDP growth remain robust, but risks from external demand and investment sentiment persist.
• Focus remains on achieving the 4% inflation target while supporting economic growth.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Monetary Policy
prelimsPoints: The RBI's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) determines the policy repo rate.
The primary objective of monetary policy is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
CPI is a measure of retail inflation.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors influencing RBI's decision to maintain the repo rate and its implications for the Indian economy.
Discuss the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control and economic growth objectives in the current global scenario.
Evaluate the effectiveness of India's inflation targeting framework and suggest potential reforms.
Examine the interplay between global geopolitical events and India's domestic economic stability and monetary policy decisions.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic depicting the RBI logo with upward and downward arrows indicating economic trends.
articles.trivia: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) in India consists of six members: three from the RBI and three external members appointed by the government.

articles.headline: India-ASEAN Strategic Dialogue Concludes, Focus on Maritime Security and Blue Economy
articles.explainer:
• The annual India-ASEAN Strategic Dialogue in New Delhi concluded with a joint statement emphasizing enhanced cooperation in maritime security.
• Key discussions revolved around freedom of navigation, regional stability in the Indo-Pacific, and combating illegal fishing.
• Both sides committed to strengthening partnerships in the 'Blue Economy' sectors, including sustainable marine resource management and coastal development.
• Agreements signed for joint research and capacity building in areas like disaster management and marine pollution control.
• The dialogue reinforced India's 'Act East' policy and ASEAN's centrality in regional architecture.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora
prelimsPoints: ASEAN consists of 10 member states.
India is a sectoral dialogue partner of ASEAN since 1992 and a strategic partner since 2012.
The 'Act East' policy was launched in 2014, succeeding the 'Look East' policy.
UNCLOS (United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea) governs maritime activities.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of India-ASEAN cooperation in ensuring regional stability and security in the Indo-Pacific.
Discuss the potential of the 'Blue Economy' partnership between India and ASEAN for sustainable development and economic growth.
Evaluate the effectiveness of India's 'Act East' policy in strengthening ties with Southeast Asian nations.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for India in navigating complex geopolitical dynamics in the South China Sea and wider Indo-Pacific region.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and ASEAN member states displayed together, with a naval vessel in the background.
articles.trivia: ASEAN was founded on August 8, 1967, in Bangkok, Thailand, with the signing of the ASEAN Declaration (Bangkok Declaration).

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Conducts Test Fire for Next-Gen Launch Vehicle (NGLV) Engine
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced the successful static test fire of the semi-cryogenic engine for its Next-Generation Launch Vehicle (NGLV).
• NGLV aims to replace the PSLV and GSLV series, offering cost-effective and heavier payload capacity for future missions.
• The engine uses a more efficient and environmentally friendly semi-cryogenic propellant combination.
• This development is crucial for India's ambitions in human spaceflight, satellite constellations, and deep-space exploration.
• NGLV is designed for reusability, significantly reducing launch costs and increasing launch frequency.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Awareness in the fields of Space
prelimsPoints: ISRO is India's national space agency.
NGLV is intended to be a reusable, heavy-lift launch vehicle.
Semi-cryogenic engines use liquid oxygen and refined kerosene (ISROSENE) as propellants.
PSLV and GSLV are current operational launch vehicles of ISRO.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of the NGLV project for India's future space endeavors and its position in the global space market.
Analyze the technological challenges and breakthroughs involved in developing semi-cryogenic engines and reusable launch vehicles.
Evaluate how NGLV can contribute to India's human spaceflight program (Gaganyaan) and other ambitious missions.
Examine the economic benefits and environmental advantages of reusable launch vehicle technology.
articles.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of the NGLV launching into space, or a close-up of a rocket engine test fire.
articles.trivia: ISRO's first successful launch of a satellite using an indigenously developed launch vehicle (SLV-3) was in 1980, placing Rohini-1 into orbit.

articles.headline: New Report Highlights Increasing Coastal Vulnerability and Salinity Intrusion in India
articles.explainer:
• A comprehensive report by the Ministry of Earth Sciences details the escalating threat of sea-level rise and coastal erosion in India.
• It warns of increased salinity intrusion into groundwater and agricultural lands, particularly in states like West Bengal, Odisha, and Gujarat.
• The report emphasizes the disproportionate impact on coastal communities, affecting livelihoods and displacing populations.
• Recommendations include integrated coastal zone management, mangrove restoration, and climate-resilient infrastructure development.
• Calls for urgent policy interventions and community-based adaptation strategies to mitigate the adverse effects.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Disaster and disaster management
prelimsPoints: India has a coastline of over 7,500 km.
Mangroves play a crucial role in protecting coastlines.
Salinity intrusion affects drinking water and agricultural productivity.
The Ministry of Earth Sciences is responsible for research on oceans, atmosphere, and climate.
mainsPoints: Analyze the socio-economic and ecological impacts of coastal vulnerability and salinity intrusion on India's coastal regions.
Discuss the effectiveness of current government policies and programs for coastal zone management and climate adaptation.
Suggest innovative strategies and technologies for building climate resilience in coastal communities, including nature-based solutions.
Examine the challenges in implementing sustainable solutions for coastal protection and the role of international cooperation.
articles.imageDescription: An aerial view of a coastline showing signs of erosion or a village affected by rising sea levels.
articles.trivia: The Sundarbans, a UNESCO World Heritage Site shared by India and Bangladesh, is the largest single block of tidal halophytic mangrove forest in the world and is highly vulnerable to sea-level rise.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Bench to Hear Petitions on Electoral Bond Scheme Transparency
articles.explainer:
• A special bench of the Supreme Court is slated to hear a batch of petitions challenging the transparency and constitutionality of the Electoral Bond Scheme.
• Petitioners argue the scheme promotes opaque political funding, violates the 'Right to Know' of citizens, and lacks accountability.
• The government maintains the scheme aims to curb black money in elections and ensure donor anonymity.
• The hearing is expected to delve into the balance between donor privacy and electoral transparency.
• The court's decision could significantly impact future political funding reforms in India.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions
prelimsPoints: The Electoral Bond Scheme was introduced in 2017.
Electoral bonds are interest-free bearer instruments.
The Supreme Court is the highest judicial body in India.
Article 19(1)(a) of the Indian Constitution guarantees freedom of speech and expression, which includes the Right to Know.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the arguments for and against the Electoral Bond Scheme in the context of electoral transparency and political funding.
Discuss how the Electoral Bond Scheme impacts the 'Right to Know' of citizens and the principles of free and fair elections.
Evaluate the role of the Supreme Court in safeguarding constitutional principles related to electoral democracy.
Suggest alternative mechanisms for political funding that promote both transparency and accountability in India.
articles.imageDescription: A gavel and legal books, symbolizing judicial proceedings, with a blurred image of ballot boxes in the background.
articles.trivia: The first electoral bonds were issued in March 2018. They can be purchased from specified branches of the State Bank of India.

articles.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission Achieves Milestone in Health ID Creation and Interoperability
articles.explainer:
• The Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) announced reaching a significant milestone in creating Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA IDs).
• The platform has successfully facilitated the digital linking of health records across a growing number of public and private healthcare providers.
• Focus on enhancing interoperability between various health systems, enabling seamless access to patient data for better care coordination.
• Challenges remain in ensuring data security, privacy, and widespread adoption, especially in rural and remote areas.
• Government aims to onboard all major healthcare facilities and citizens onto the ABDM ecosystem by 2030.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
articles.source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
prelimsPoints: ABDM was launched in 2021.
It aims to create a digital health ecosystem in India.
ABHA ID is a 14-digit unique health identification number.
National Health Authority (NHA) is the implementing agency for ABDM.
mainsPoints: Analyze the potential benefits and challenges of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission in transforming India's healthcare landscape.
Discuss the role of digital health IDs and interoperable systems in improving access to healthcare and reducing inequalities.
Examine the ethical considerations and data privacy concerns associated with the creation and sharing of digital health records.
Suggest measures to accelerate the adoption of ABDM in rural and underserved areas, ensuring inclusive access to digital health services.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized graphic showing a network of healthcare facilities connected digitally with patient data flowing between them.
articles.trivia: The Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY), launched in 2018, is the world's largest government-funded health insurance scheme.

articles.headline: India Positioned as Key Player in Building Global Supply Chain Resilience
articles.explainer:
• India's growing manufacturing capabilities and strategic location are positioning it as a crucial hub for global supply chain diversification.
• Efforts to attract foreign investment through 'Make in India' and PLI schemes are yielding results in critical sectors.
• Discussions at international forums highlight the need for resilient supply chains post-pandemic and geopolitical disruptions.
• India is actively engaging in bilateral and multilateral initiatives to foster secure and diversified trade routes.
• Focus on developing robust logistics infrastructure and skilled workforce to capitalize on this opportunity.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: The PLI scheme aims to boost domestic manufacturing in various sectors.
India is part of the Supply Chain Resilience Initiative (SCRI) with Japan and Australia.
FDI is a major source of non-debt financial resources for India's economic development.
'Make in India' initiative was launched in 2014.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's strategic advantages and policy initiatives that are contributing to its emergence as a global supply chain hub.
Discuss the challenges India faces in fully realizing its potential in global manufacturing and supply chain diversification.
Evaluate the impact of schemes like 'Make in India' and PLI on enhancing domestic production and attracting foreign investment.
Examine the geopolitical implications of building resilient supply chains and India's role in shaping future trade architectures.
articles.imageDescription: A world map with interconnected supply routes, highlighting India's central position.
articles.trivia: The term 'supply chain' was first coined by Keith Oliver, a consultant at Booz Allen Hamilton, in 1982.

articles.headline: G20 Sherpas Discuss Progress on Sustainable Development Goals Ahead of Next Summit
articles.explainer:
• G20 Sherpas convened to review progress on commitments towards the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), particularly in developing nations.
• Discussions focused on accelerating action on climate finance, food security, and digital public infrastructure for inclusive growth.
• India, as a significant voice from the Global South, emphasized the need for equitable partnerships and technology transfer.
• Preparations are underway for the upcoming G20 Leaders' Summit, aiming for concrete outcomes and actionable plans.
• Challenges include geopolitical fragmentation and the widening SDG financing gap, requiring concerted global efforts.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Important International institutions, agencies and fora - their structure, mandate
prelimsPoints: The G20 is an intergovernmental forum comprising 19 countries and the European Union.
There are 17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) adopted by the UN in 2015.
Climate finance refers to local, national or transnational financing for climate action.
Sherpas are personal representatives of the leaders of their respective countries at international summits.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's role and contributions to the G20's agenda on sustainable development and climate action.
Discuss the major challenges in achieving the Sustainable Development Goals by 2030, particularly for developing countries.
Evaluate the effectiveness of global forums like the G20 in mobilizing climate finance and fostering international cooperation.
Examine the concept of Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) and its potential to accelerate SDG achievement in the Global South.
articles.imageDescription: A collage of G20 country flags with the SDG wheel overlaid.
articles.trivia: The G20 accounts for around 85% of global GDP, over 75% of global trade, and about two-thirds of the world population.

articles.headline: PM Vishwakarma Scheme Shows Promising Results in Empowering Artisans and Craftspeople
articles.explainer:
• A progress report on the PM Vishwakarma Scheme indicates positive outcomes in upskilling traditional artisans and craftspeople across India.
• The scheme provides financial assistance, modern tools, and market linkages to 'Vishwakarmas' (artisans) in 18 traditional trades.
• Emphasis on preserving traditional arts and crafts while integrating them with modern technology and market demands.
• Data shows increased income levels and enhanced entrepreneurial opportunities for beneficiaries.
• Government plans to expand the scheme's reach and introduce more trades under its ambit.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Education, Human Resources
articles.source: Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship
prelimsPoints: PM Vishwakarma Scheme was launched in 2023.
It focuses on artisans and craftspeople involved in 18 traditional trades.
The scheme provides training, toolkits, credit support, and market linkages.
It aims to integrate 'Vishwakarmas' into the formal economy.
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and key features of the PM Vishwakarma Scheme and its potential impact on India's traditional artisan community.
Discuss how such schemes contribute to the preservation of cultural heritage and the promotion of local economies.
Evaluate the challenges faced by traditional artisans in accessing modern markets and technology, and how the scheme addresses them.
Suggest further policy measures to strengthen the MSME sector and promote entrepreneurship among 'Vishwakarmas'.
articles.imageDescription: Images of various traditional Indian crafts and artisans at work, with the scheme logo.
articles.trivia: The scheme's name 'Vishwakarma' refers to the divine architect and craftsman in Hindu mythology, symbolizing creativity and skill.

articles.headline: India on Track to Exceed Renewable Energy Capacity Targets by 2030, Says Ministry Report
articles.explainer:
• A recent report by the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy indicates that India is well-positioned to surpass its non-fossil fuel capacity targets ahead of 2030.
• Aggressive deployment of solar and wind energy projects, coupled with policy support, drives this growth.
• The country aims for 500 GW of non-fossil fuel-based energy capacity by 2030, a key commitment at COP26.
• Challenges include grid integration, land acquisition, and ensuring stable power supply from intermittent sources.
• Focus on green hydrogen and energy storage solutions to further accelerate the transition.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: India's target is 500 GW of non-fossil fuel-based energy capacity by 2030.
COP26 (Glasgow) saw India announce enhanced climate commitments.
The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) is the nodal ministry for renewable energy.
Solar and wind power are major components of India's renewable energy mix.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to India's rapid growth in renewable energy capacity and its implications for climate change mitigation.
Discuss the challenges associated with integrating large-scale renewable energy into the national grid and ensuring energy security.
Evaluate the potential of green hydrogen as a key enabler for India's energy transition and its economic implications.
Examine the policy and regulatory frameworks needed to further accelerate renewable energy deployment and investment in India.
articles.imageDescription: A vast solar power plant or a wind farm against a blue sky, symbolizing renewable energy.
articles.trivia: India is home to the world's largest single-location solar power project, the Bhadla Solar Park in Rajasthan.

articles.headline: New Initiatives Launched to Boost Heritage Tourism and Local Economies
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Tourism unveiled a series of new initiatives aimed at promoting heritage tourism and generating local employment.
• Focus on developing lesser-known historical sites, improving infrastructure, and enhancing visitor experience through digital interventions.
• Schemes include skill development programs for local guides and artisans, and promotion of homestays near heritage locations.
• Emphasis on sustainable tourism practices to preserve cultural heritage while ensuring economic benefits for local communities.
• Collaborations with state governments and private stakeholders for integrated development of tourism circuits.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India
prelimsPoints: The Ministry of Tourism is responsible for promoting tourism in India.
PRASHAD Scheme focuses on pilgrimage rejuvenation and spiritual heritage augmentation.
Swadesh Darshan Scheme aims at integrated development of theme-based tourist circuits.
India has numerous UNESCO World Heritage Sites.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of heritage tourism in fostering cultural preservation, economic growth, and community empowerment in India.
Analyze the challenges in developing and managing heritage sites while balancing conservation with tourism promotion.
Evaluate the role of government schemes and public-private partnerships in enhancing India's tourism infrastructure and visitor experience.
Examine the concept of sustainable and responsible tourism in the context of India's diverse cultural and natural heritage.
articles.imageDescription: A collage of iconic Indian heritage sites like historical monuments and temples.
articles.trivia: Agra Fort, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, was originally a brick fort held by the Chauhan Rajputs before being captured and rebuilt by Emperor Akbar.

articles.headline: Indus Water Treaty: India-Pakistan Permanent Indus Commission Convenes
articles.explainer:
• The Permanent Indus Commission, comprising water commissioners from India and Pakistan, held its annual meeting to discuss issues related to the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT).
• Discussions focused on technical matters concerning various hydroelectric projects on the western rivers and data exchange.
• Both sides reiterated their commitment to the provisions of the treaty, despite occasional political tensions.
• The IWT, brokered by the World Bank, is considered one of the most successful water-sharing agreements globally.
• Addressing concerns over new projects and ensuring transparent information sharing remains key to the treaty's continued success.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: The Indus Water Treaty was signed in 1960.
It was brokered by the World Bank.
The treaty allocates the Eastern Rivers to India and the Western Rivers to Pakistan.
The Permanent Indus Commission is mandated to meet at least once a year.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the Indus Water Treaty as a model for transboundary water sharing and its role in India-Pakistan relations.
Discuss the challenges faced by the Permanent Indus Commission in resolving technical disputes and ensuring adherence to the treaty's provisions.
Evaluate the implications of climate change and increasing water scarcity on the long-term viability and effectiveness of the IWT.
Examine India's rights and responsibilities under the IWT concerning the development of hydroelectric projects on the Western Rivers.
articles.imageDescription: A map showing the Indus River system with India and Pakistan borders, or a handshake over a river.
articles.trivia: The Indus Water Treaty is often cited as a successful example of conflict resolution and cooperation between two historically adversarial nations.

articles.headline: National Education Policy: Focus on Integrating Vocational Training into Mainstream Curriculum
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education highlighted accelerated efforts to integrate vocational education and skill development into mainstream school and higher education curricula.
• This aligns with the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020's vision for holistic and skill-oriented learning.
• Pilot programs show positive results in enhancing employability and reducing the skill gap among youth.
• Challenges include developing standardized vocational curricula, training qualified instructors, and establishing industry linkages.
• The goal is to make vocational education accessible and appealing to a wider student base, starting from middle school.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Education, Human Resources
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 replaced the 1986 policy.
NEP 2020 aims for universalization of education from pre-school to secondary level.
It promotes vocational education integration from Class 6 onwards.
The policy emphasizes multidisciplinary education.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key provisions of NEP 2020 regarding vocational education and its potential to transform India's education system.
Discuss the challenges in effectively integrating vocational training into mainstream schooling and higher education in India.
Evaluate the role of industry partnerships and skill development initiatives in enhancing the employability of Indian youth.
Suggest policy recommendations to overcome barriers to vocational education and promote a skill-based economy in India.
articles.imageDescription: Students engaged in hands-on vocational training, such as carpentry or electronics, in a classroom setting.
articles.trivia: India's first National Policy on Education was formulated in 1968, based on the recommendations of the Kothari Commission.

articles.headline: Animal Welfare Board Proposes Stricter Regulations for Pet Breeding and Sale
articles.explainer:
• The Animal Welfare Board of India (AWBI) has proposed new, stringent regulations for the breeding, housing, and sale of companion animals.
• Aimed at curbing illegal pet trade, preventing cruelty, and ensuring ethical treatment of animals across the country.
• The draft rules mandate registration of breeders and pet shops, regular inspections, and adherence to minimum standards of care.
• Public feedback is being sought on the proposed regulations, with strong support from animal rights organizations.
• This move follows increasing concerns over puppy mills and inhumane conditions in unregulated pet markets.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
keyTerms: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960
prelimsPoints: The Animal Welfare Board of India (AWBI) is a statutory advisory body.
It was established under the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960.
AWBI advises the government on animal welfare laws and rules.

The 42nd Amendment Act added Article 48A (protection and improvement of environment and safeguarding of forests and wild life) and Article 51A(g) (fundamental duty to protect and improve the natural environment) to the Indian Constitution.
mainsPoints: Discuss the need for stricter regulations in the pet breeding and sale industry in India, citing instances of animal cruelty and exploitation.
Analyze the role of the Animal Welfare Board of India in promoting ethical treatment of animals and enforcing existing laws.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing and monitoring animal welfare regulations across a diverse and largely unregulated sector.
Examine the ethical dimensions of human-animal interaction and the societal responsibility towards animal welfare.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic depicting a happy pet animal alongside the AWBI logo and regulations.
articles.trivia: The Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960, was enacted to prevent the infliction of unnecessary pain or suffering on animals.

articles.headline: India Accelerates Chip Manufacturing Ambitions with New Fab Proposals
articles.explainer:
• India's efforts to establish a robust semiconductor manufacturing ecosystem gain momentum with several new proposals for chip fabrication units.
• The 'India Semiconductor Mission' provides incentives and support for companies investing in local chip production.
• This strategic push aims to reduce reliance on global supply chains and enhance technological self-reliance.
• Challenges include securing advanced technology transfer, attracting skilled talent, and high initial investment costs.
• Government is focusing on both mature nodes and advanced packaging technologies to cater to diverse industry needs.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: The India Semiconductor Mission (ISM) was launched in 2021.
Semiconductors are essential components in almost all electronic devices.
The government offers incentives to attract investments in semiconductor manufacturing.
A 'fab' (fabrication plant) is a facility where integrated circuits (ICs) are manufactured.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of developing indigenous semiconductor manufacturing capabilities for India's economic and national security.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities associated with establishing a semiconductor ecosystem in India, including technological, financial, and human resource aspects.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government policies, such as the India Semiconductor Mission and PLI schemes, in attracting investment and fostering innovation.
Examine the potential geopolitical implications of India's entry into the global chip manufacturing landscape and its impact on supply chain resilience.
articles.imageDescription: A microchip or a semiconductor wafer, symbolizing advanced technology.
articles.trivia: The first integrated circuit (chip) was invented by Jack Kilby of Texas Instruments in 1958, for which he later shared the Nobel Prize in Physics.

articles.headline: Namami Gange Phase-II: Focus Shifts to Tributary Rejuvenation and Sustainable River Basin Management
articles.explainer:
• The second phase of the Namami Gange Programme intensifies efforts, extending its scope beyond the main stem of the Ganga to its tributaries.
• New projects sanctioned for pollution abatement, ecological restoration, and conservation of biodiversity in Yamuna, Ramganga, and other tributaries.
• Emphasis on sustainable river basin management, including afforestation, wetland conservation, and promotion of organic farming in catchment areas.
• Community participation and public awareness campaigns are being scaled up to ensure long-term success.
• Challenges include managing industrial and agricultural pollution, and ensuring inter-state coordination for tributary management.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Disaster and disaster management
prelimsPoints: Namami Gange Programme was launched in 2014.
It is an integrated conservation mission to abate pollution, conserve and rejuvenate the National River Ganga.
The National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG) is the implementing agency.
Ganga is India's national river.
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and achievements of the Namami Gange Programme Phase-I and the rationale behind extending it to tributaries in Phase-II.
Discuss the multi-faceted challenges in river basin management in India, particularly for large river systems like the Ganga and its tributaries.
Evaluate the role of various stakeholders, including government bodies, local communities, and NGOs, in river rejuvenation efforts.
Suggest innovative approaches and policy interventions for ensuring the sustainable health and ecological flow of India's rivers.
articles.imageDescription: A clean stretch of the Ganga River with lush green banks, or a map highlighting Ganga and its tributaries.
articles.trivia: The Ganga Action Plan, launched in 1986, was the first major initiative to clean the river Ganga.

highlights: Human Rights Day 2025 observed globally, with India reaffirming its commitments amidst evolving challenges like digital rights and climate justice.
Parliament's Winter Session debates crucial amendments to the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, aiming to balance privacy with national security.
RBI maintains repo rate steady, citing easing inflation but cautioning on global volatility, while projecting robust GDP growth for India.
India-ASEAN Strategic Dialogue concludes with enhanced cooperation in maritime security, Blue Economy, and Indo-Pacific stability.
ISRO successfully test fires the semi-cryogenic engine for its Next-Generation Launch Vehicle (NGLV), signaling a leap in space capabilities.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the Human Rights Day, consider the following statements:
1. Human Rights Day is observed on December 10th each year to commemorate the adoption of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR).
2. The UDHR was adopted by the United Nations Security Council in 1948.
3. India is a signatory to the UDHR and several other international human rights covenants.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: Human Rights Day is indeed observed on December 10th to mark the adoption of the UDHR. Statement 2 is incorrect: The UDHR was adopted by the United Nations General Assembly, not the Security Council, in 1948. Statement 3 is correct: India is a signatory to the UDHR and many other international human rights instruments.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following bodies is responsible for determining the policy repo rate in India?
options: (b) Reserve Bank of India (RBI) Board of Directors
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is a six-member body constituted by the Central Government to determine the policy repo rate required to achieve the inflation target. Three members are from the RBI and three are external members appointed by the government.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA), 2023:
1. The Act aims to regulate the processing of digital personal data in India.
2. It mandates that all personal data of Indian citizens must be stored only within India.
3. The Act defines 'Data Fiduciary' as an entity that determines the purpose and means of processing personal data.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The DPDPA, 2023, is indeed enacted to regulate the processing of digital personal data. Statement 2 is incorrect: While the initial draft had strong data localization clauses, the final Act permits cross-border transfer of data to specified countries and territories, subject to certain conditions, rather than mandating storage only within India. Statement 3 is correct: The Act defines 'Data Fiduciary' as any person who alone or in conjunction with other persons determines the purpose and means of processing of personal data.

prelimsMocks.question: With respect to India's space program, what does NGLV stand for?
prelimsMocks.explanation: NGLV stands for Next-Generation Launch Vehicle, which is ISRO's planned heavy-lift, reusable rocket designed to replace the PSLV and GSLV series.

prelimsMocks.question: The Indus Water Treaty (IWT) was signed between India and Pakistan in 1960. Which international organization brokered this treaty?
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Indus Water Treaty, a water-distribution treaty between India and Pakistan, was brokered by the World Bank (then the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development) and signed in Karachi on September 19, 1960.

mainsMocks.question: Critically analyze India's progress in upholding human rights since the adoption of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR). Discuss the evolving challenges, particularly in the digital age, and suggest measures for strengthening the human rights framework in the country.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Human Rights; Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce Human Rights Day and UDHR. Discuss India's constitutional guarantees (Fundamental Rights, DPSP) and legal framework (NHRC). Analyze progress and achievements, then delve into challenges like digital rights, privacy, climate justice, and protection of vulnerable groups. Conclude with forward-looking policy recommendations.

mainsMocks.question: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, aims to create a robust data privacy framework in India. Discuss the key provisions of the Act and the challenges in its effective implementation, particularly concerning balancing individual privacy with national security and economic interests.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation; GS3: Cyber security.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce DPDPA and its objectives. Explain key provisions like data fiduciary, data principal rights, consent, cross-border data transfer, and penalties. Analyze implementation challenges (technological, legal, infrastructure, awareness, balancing competing interests). Conclude with suggestions for a balanced and effective data governance model.

mainsMocks.question: India's ambition to become a global manufacturing hub hinges on building resilient supply chains and fostering a robust semiconductor ecosystem. Evaluate the government's initiatives in this regard and discuss the opportunities and challenges for India to achieve technological self-reliance in critical sectors.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment; Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth; Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
mainsMocks.approach: Start with India's vision for manufacturing and supply chain resilience (e.g., Atmanirbhar Bharat, Make in India). Detail initiatives like PLI schemes, India Semiconductor Mission. Discuss opportunities (large domestic market, skilled workforce, geopolitical shifts). Address challenges (technology gaps, capital intensity, infrastructure, talent retention). Conclude with strategic recommendations for fostering self-reliance.

quickBytes.text: Human Rights Day is celebrated on December 10, commemorating the UDHR adoption in 1948.
The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, regulates digital personal data processing in India.
RBI's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) sets the policy repo rate to manage inflation and growth.
ISRO's NGLV (Next-Generation Launch Vehicle) aims for reusable, heavy-lift space launches.
India targets 500 GW non-fossil fuel energy capacity by 2030, a COP26 commitment.
The Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) creates ABHA IDs for digital health records.
PM Vishwakarma Scheme supports traditional artisans with skill development and financial aid.
Indus Water Treaty (1960) between India and Pakistan was brokered by the World Bank.
National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 emphasizes integrating vocational education from Class 6.
India Semiconductor Mission aims to build a robust domestic chip manufacturing ecosystem.

GS1.headline: New Initiatives Launched to Boost Heritage Tourism and Local Economies
GS1.explainer:
• The Ministry of Tourism unveiled a series of new initiatives aimed at promoting heritage tourism and generating local employment.
• Focus on developing lesser-known historical sites, improving infrastructure, and enhancing visitor experience through digital interventions.
• Schemes include skill development programs for local guides and artisans, and promotion of homestays near heritage locations.
• Emphasis on sustainable tourism practices to preserve cultural heritage while ensuring economic benefits for local communities.
• Collaborations with state governments and private stakeholders for integrated development of tourism circuits.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India
prelimsPoints: The Ministry of Tourism is responsible for promoting tourism in India.
PRASHAD Scheme focuses on pilgrimage rejuvenation and spiritual heritage augmentation.
Swadesh Darshan Scheme aims at integrated development of theme-based tourist circuits.
India has numerous UNESCO World Heritage Sites.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of heritage tourism in fostering cultural preservation, economic growth, and community empowerment in India.
Analyze the challenges in developing and managing heritage sites while balancing conservation with tourism promotion.
Evaluate the role of government schemes and public-private partnerships in enhancing India's tourism infrastructure and visitor experience.
Examine the concept of sustainable and responsible tourism in the context of India's diverse cultural and natural heritage.
GS1.imageDescription: A collage of iconic Indian heritage sites like historical monuments and temples.
GS1.trivia: Agra Fort, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, was originally a brick fort held by the Chauhan Rajputs before being captured and rebuilt by Emperor Akbar.

GS2.headline: Human Rights Day 2025: India Reaffirms Commitments Amidst Evolving Challenges
GS2.explainer:
• Celebrated globally on December 10th, marking the adoption of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) in 1948.
• India commemorates Human Rights Day by reflecting on its constitutional guarantees and international obligations.
• Focus this year includes digital rights, climate justice, and protection of vulnerable groups in a rapidly changing world.
• Discussions center on balancing national security with individual liberties, particularly in the context of emerging technologies.
• NHRC and civil society organizations highlight areas for improvement in human rights protection and awareness.
gsSubTopics: Human Rights
Constitutional provisions
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population
prelimsPoints: Human Rights Day is observed on December 10th.
The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) was adopted by the UN General Assembly on December 10, 1948.
India is a signatory to various international human rights covenants.
The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) is a statutory body in India.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's progress and challenges in upholding human rights since the adoption of the UDHR.
Discuss the role of the NHRC and civil society in promoting and protecting human rights in India.
Examine the impact of technological advancements (e.g., AI, surveillance) on human rights and the need for new legal frameworks.
Evaluate the intersection of climate change and human rights, particularly for marginalized communities in India.
GS2.imageDescription: A graphic symbolizing human rights, possibly featuring the UDHR scroll and diverse hands reaching out.
GS2.trivia: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights holds the Guinness World Record as the most translated document in the world, available in over 500 languages.

GS2.headline: Parliament Winter Session: Key Amendments to Digital Personal Data Protection Act Debated
GS2.explainer:
• The Winter Session of Parliament sees intense debate over proposed amendments to the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.
• Amendments aim to address concerns raised by industry stakeholders and privacy advocates regarding data localization and consent mechanisms.
• Focus on striking a balance between data innovation, national security, and individual privacy rights.
• Opposition parties demand clearer definitions for 'significant data fiduciaries' and the scope of government exemptions.
• The Joint Parliamentary Committee report on the proposed changes is expected to be tabled soon.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Information Technology
prelimsPoints: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act was enacted in 2023.
The Act aims to regulate the processing of digital personal data.
It defines 'Data Fiduciary' and 'Data Principal'.
Parliamentary sessions include Monsoon, Winter, and Budget sessions.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, and the necessity for its amendments.
Discuss the challenges in implementing a robust data protection framework in India, considering technological advancements and economic interests.
Analyze the implications of data localization mandates on global businesses and India's digital economy aspirations.
Examine the balance between national security concerns and individual privacy rights in the context of digital data.
GS2.imageDescription: An illustration of the Indian Parliament building with digital data streams flowing around it.
GS2.trivia: India's first attempt at a comprehensive data protection law began with the Justice B.N. Srikrishna Committee report in 2018.

GS2.headline: India-ASEAN Strategic Dialogue Concludes, Focus on Maritime Security and Blue Economy
GS2.explainer:
• The annual India-ASEAN Strategic Dialogue in New Delhi concluded with a joint statement emphasizing enhanced cooperation in maritime security.
• Key discussions revolved around freedom of navigation, regional stability in the Indo-Pacific, and combating illegal fishing.
• Both sides committed to strengthening partnerships in the 'Blue Economy' sectors, including sustainable marine resource management and coastal development.
• Agreements signed for joint research and capacity building in areas like disaster management and marine pollution control.
• The dialogue reinforced India's 'Act East' policy and ASEAN's centrality in regional architecture.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora
prelimsPoints: ASEAN consists of 10 member states.
India is a sectoral dialogue partner of ASEAN since 1992 and a strategic partner since 2012.
The 'Act East' policy was launched in 2014, succeeding the 'Look East' policy.
UNCLOS (United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea) governs maritime activities.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of India-ASEAN cooperation in ensuring regional stability and security in the Indo-Pacific.
Discuss the potential of the 'Blue Economy' partnership between India and ASEAN for sustainable development and economic growth.
Evaluate the effectiveness of India's 'Act East' policy in strengthening ties with Southeast Asian nations.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for India in navigating complex geopolitical dynamics in the South China Sea and wider Indo-Pacific region.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and ASEAN member states displayed together, with a naval vessel in the background.
GS2.trivia: ASEAN was founded on August 8, 1967, in Bangkok, Thailand, with the signing of the ASEAN Declaration (Bangkok Declaration).

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Bench to Hear Petitions on Electoral Bond Scheme Transparency
GS2.explainer:
• A special bench of the Supreme Court is slated to hear a batch of petitions challenging the transparency and constitutionality of the Electoral Bond Scheme.
• Petitioners argue the scheme promotes opaque political funding, violates the 'Right to Know' of citizens, and lacks accountability.
• The government maintains the scheme aims to curb black money in elections and ensure donor anonymity.
• The hearing is expected to delve into the balance between donor privacy and electoral transparency.
• The court's decision could significantly impact future political funding reforms in India.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions
prelimsPoints: The Electoral Bond Scheme was introduced in 2017.
Electoral bonds are interest-free bearer instruments.
The Supreme Court is the highest judicial body in India.
Article 19(1)(a) of the Indian Constitution guarantees freedom of speech and expression, which includes the Right to Know.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the arguments for and against the Electoral Bond Scheme in the context of electoral transparency and political funding.
Discuss how the Electoral Bond Scheme impacts the 'Right to Know' of citizens and the principles of free and fair elections.
Evaluate the role of the Supreme Court in safeguarding constitutional principles related to electoral democracy.
Suggest alternative mechanisms for political funding that promote both transparency and accountability in India.
GS2.imageDescription: A gavel and legal books, symbolizing judicial proceedings, with a blurred image of ballot boxes in the background.
GS2.trivia: The first electoral bonds were issued in March 2018. They can be purchased from specified branches of the State Bank of India.

GS2.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission Achieves Milestone in Health ID Creation and Interoperability
GS2.explainer:
• The Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) announced reaching a significant milestone in creating Ayushman Bharat Health Accounts (ABHA IDs).
• The platform has successfully facilitated the digital linking of health records across a growing number of public and private healthcare providers.
• Focus on enhancing interoperability between various health systems, enabling seamless access to patient data for better care coordination.
• Challenges remain in ensuring data security, privacy, and widespread adoption, especially in rural and remote areas.
• Government aims to onboard all major healthcare facilities and citizens onto the ABDM ecosystem by 2030.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
GS2.source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
prelimsPoints: ABDM was launched in 2021.
It aims to create a digital health ecosystem in India.
ABHA ID is a 14-digit unique health identification number.
National Health Authority (NHA) is the implementing agency for ABDM.
mainsPoints: Analyze the potential benefits and challenges of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission in transforming India's healthcare landscape.
Discuss the role of digital health IDs and interoperable systems in improving access to healthcare and reducing inequalities.
Examine the ethical considerations and data privacy concerns associated with the creation and sharing of digital health records.
Suggest measures to accelerate the adoption of ABDM in rural and underserved areas, ensuring inclusive access to digital health services.
GS2.imageDescription: A stylized graphic showing a network of healthcare facilities connected digitally with patient data flowing between them.
GS2.trivia: The Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY), launched in 2018, is the world's largest government-funded health insurance scheme.

GS2.headline: G20 Sherpas Discuss Progress on Sustainable Development Goals Ahead of Next Summit
GS2.explainer:
• G20 Sherpas convened to review progress on commitments towards the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), particularly in developing nations.
• Discussions focused on accelerating action on climate finance, food security, and digital public infrastructure for inclusive growth.
• India, as a significant voice from the Global South, emphasized the need for equitable partnerships and technology transfer.
• Preparations are underway for the upcoming G20 Leaders' Summit, aiming for concrete outcomes and actionable plans.
• Challenges include geopolitical fragmentation and the widening SDG financing gap, requiring concerted global efforts.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Important International institutions, agencies and fora - their structure, mandate
prelimsPoints: The G20 is an intergovernmental forum comprising 19 countries and the European Union.
There are 17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) adopted by the UN in 2015.
Climate finance refers to local, national or transnational financing for climate action.
Sherpas are personal representatives of the leaders of their respective countries at international summits.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's role and contributions to the G20's agenda on sustainable development and climate action.
Discuss the major challenges in achieving the Sustainable Development Goals by 2030, particularly for developing countries.
Evaluate the effectiveness of global forums like the G20 in mobilizing climate finance and fostering international cooperation.
Examine the concept of Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) and its potential to accelerate SDG achievement in the Global South.
GS2.imageDescription: A collage of G20 country flags with the SDG wheel overlaid.
GS2.trivia: The G20 accounts for around 85% of global GDP, over 75% of global trade, and about two-thirds of the world population.

GS2.headline: PM Vishwakarma Scheme Shows Promising Results in Empowering Artisans and Craftspeople
GS2.explainer:
• A progress report on the PM Vishwakarma Scheme indicates positive outcomes in upskilling traditional artisans and craftspeople across India.
• The scheme provides financial assistance, modern tools, and market linkages to 'Vishwakarmas' (artisans) in 18 traditional trades.
• Emphasis on preserving traditional arts and crafts while integrating them with modern technology and market demands.
• Data shows increased income levels and enhanced entrepreneurial opportunities for beneficiaries.
• Government plans to expand the scheme's reach and introduce more trades under its ambit.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Education, Human Resources
GS2.source: Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship
prelimsPoints: PM Vishwakarma Scheme was launched in 2023.
It focuses on artisans and craftspeople involved in 18 traditional trades.
The scheme provides training, toolkits, credit support, and market linkages.
It aims to integrate 'Vishwakarmas' into the formal economy.
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and key features of the PM Vishwakarma Scheme and its potential impact on India's traditional artisan community.
Discuss how such schemes contribute to the preservation of cultural heritage and the promotion of local economies.
Evaluate the challenges faced by traditional artisans in accessing modern markets and technology, and how the scheme addresses them.
Suggest further policy measures to strengthen the MSME sector and promote entrepreneurship among 'Vishwakarmas'.
GS2.imageDescription: Images of various traditional Indian crafts and artisans at work, with the scheme logo.
GS2.trivia: The scheme's name 'Vishwakarma' refers to the divine architect and craftsman in Hindu mythology, symbolizing creativity and skill.

GS2.headline: Indus Water Treaty: India-Pakistan Permanent Indus Commission Convenes
GS2.explainer:
• The Permanent Indus Commission, comprising water commissioners from India and Pakistan, held its annual meeting to discuss issues related to the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT).
• Discussions focused on technical matters concerning various hydroelectric projects on the western rivers and data exchange.
• Both sides reiterated their commitment to the provisions of the treaty, despite occasional political tensions.
• The IWT, brokered by the World Bank, is considered one of the most successful water-sharing agreements globally.
• Addressing concerns over new projects and ensuring transparent information sharing remains key to the treaty's continued success.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: The Indus Water Treaty was signed in 1960.
It was brokered by the World Bank.
The treaty allocates the Eastern Rivers to India and the Western Rivers to Pakistan.
The Permanent Indus Commission is mandated to meet at least once a year.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the Indus Water Treaty as a model for transboundary water sharing and its role in India-Pakistan relations.
Discuss the challenges faced by the Permanent Indus Commission in resolving technical disputes and ensuring adherence to the treaty's provisions.
Evaluate the implications of climate change and increasing water scarcity on the long-term viability and effectiveness of the IWT.
Examine India's rights and responsibilities under the IWT concerning the development of hydroelectric projects on the Western Rivers.
GS2.imageDescription: A map showing the Indus River system with India and Pakistan borders, or a handshake over a river.
GS2.trivia: The Indus Water Treaty is often cited as a successful example of conflict resolution and cooperation between two historically adversarial nations.

GS2.headline: National Education Policy: Focus on Integrating Vocational Training into Mainstream Curriculum
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education highlighted accelerated efforts to integrate vocational education and skill development into mainstream school and higher education curricula.
• This aligns with the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020's vision for holistic and skill-oriented learning.
• Pilot programs show positive results in enhancing employability and reducing the skill gap among youth.
• Challenges include developing standardized vocational curricula, training qualified instructors, and establishing industry linkages.
• The goal is to make vocational education accessible and appealing to a wider student base, starting from middle school.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Education, Human Resources
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
prelimsPoints: The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 replaced the 1986 policy.
NEP 2020 aims for universalization of education from pre-school to secondary level.
It promotes vocational education integration from Class 6 onwards.
The policy emphasizes multidisciplinary education.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key provisions of NEP 2020 regarding vocational education and its potential to transform India's education system.
Discuss the challenges in effectively integrating vocational training into mainstream schooling and higher education in India.
Evaluate the role of industry partnerships and skill development initiatives in enhancing the employability of Indian youth.
Suggest policy recommendations to overcome barriers to vocational education and promote a skill-based economy in India.
GS2.imageDescription: Students engaged in hands-on vocational training, such as carpentry or electronics, in a classroom setting.
GS2.trivia: India's first National Policy on Education was formulated in 1968, based on the recommendations of the Kothari Commission.

GS3.headline: RBI Holds Repo Rate Steady Amidst Easing Inflation, Cautions on Global Volatility
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) maintained the repo rate at its current level for the sixth consecutive review.
• Decision driven by a cautious approach, despite a marginal easing of retail inflation (CPI) in recent months.
• RBI Governor highlighted global economic uncertainties, geopolitical tensions, and potential impact on commodity prices.
• Forecasts for India's GDP growth remain robust, but risks from external demand and investment sentiment persist.
• Focus remains on achieving the 4% inflation target while supporting economic growth.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Monetary Policy
prelimsPoints: The RBI's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) determines the policy repo rate.
The primary objective of monetary policy is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
CPI is a measure of retail inflation.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors influencing RBI's decision to maintain the repo rate and its implications for the Indian economy.
Discuss the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control and economic growth objectives in the current global scenario.
Evaluate the effectiveness of India's inflation targeting framework and suggest potential reforms.
Examine the interplay between global geopolitical events and India's domestic economic stability and monetary policy decisions.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic depicting the RBI logo with upward and downward arrows indicating economic trends.
GS3.trivia: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) in India consists of six members: three from the RBI and three external members appointed by the government.

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Conducts Test Fire for Next-Gen Launch Vehicle (NGLV) Engine
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced the successful static test fire of the semi-cryogenic engine for its Next-Generation Launch Vehicle (NGLV).
• NGLV aims to replace the PSLV and GSLV series, offering cost-effective and heavier payload capacity for future missions.
• The engine uses a more efficient and environmentally friendly semi-cryogenic propellant combination.
• This development is crucial for India's ambitions in human spaceflight, satellite constellations, and deep-space exploration.
• NGLV is designed for reusability, significantly reducing launch costs and increasing launch frequency.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Awareness in the fields of Space
prelimsPoints: ISRO is India's national space agency.
NGLV is intended to be a reusable, heavy-lift launch vehicle.
Semi-cryogenic engines use liquid oxygen and refined kerosene (ISROSENE) as propellants.
PSLV and GSLV are current operational launch vehicles of ISRO.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of the NGLV project for India's future space endeavors and its position in the global space market.
Analyze the technological challenges and breakthroughs involved in developing semi-cryogenic engines and reusable launch vehicles.
Evaluate how NGLV can contribute to India's human spaceflight program (Gaganyaan) and other ambitious missions.
Examine the economic benefits and environmental advantages of reusable launch vehicle technology.
GS3.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of the NGLV launching into space, or a close-up of a rocket engine test fire.
GS3.trivia: ISRO's first successful launch of a satellite using an indigenously developed launch vehicle (SLV-3) was in 1980, placing Rohini-1 into orbit.

GS3.headline: New Report Highlights Increasing Coastal Vulnerability and Salinity Intrusion in India
GS3.explainer:
• A comprehensive report by the Ministry of Earth Sciences details the escalating threat of sea-level rise and coastal erosion in India.
• It warns of increased salinity intrusion into groundwater and agricultural lands, particularly in states like West Bengal, Odisha, and Gujarat.
• The report emphasizes the disproportionate impact on coastal communities, affecting livelihoods and displacing populations.
• Recommendations include integrated coastal zone management, mangrove restoration, and climate-resilient infrastructure development.
• Calls for urgent policy interventions and community-based adaptation strategies to mitigate the adverse effects.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Disaster and disaster management
prelimsPoints: India has a coastline of over 7,500 km.
Mangroves play a crucial role in protecting coastlines.
Salinity intrusion affects drinking water and agricultural productivity.
The Ministry of Earth Sciences is responsible for research on oceans, atmosphere, and climate.
mainsPoints: Analyze the socio-economic and ecological impacts of coastal vulnerability and salinity intrusion on India's coastal regions.
Discuss the effectiveness of current government policies and programs for coastal zone management and climate adaptation.
Suggest innovative strategies and technologies for building climate resilience in coastal communities, including nature-based solutions.
Examine the challenges in implementing sustainable solutions for coastal protection and the role of international cooperation.
GS3.imageDescription: An aerial view of a coastline showing signs of erosion or a village affected by rising sea levels.
GS3.trivia: The Sundarbans, a UNESCO World Heritage Site shared by India and Bangladesh, is the largest single block of tidal halophytic mangrove forest in the world and is highly vulnerable to sea-level rise.

GS3.headline: India Positioned as Key Player in Building Global Supply Chain Resilience
GS3.explainer:
• India's growing manufacturing capabilities and strategic location are positioning it as a crucial hub for global supply chain diversification.
• Efforts to attract foreign investment through 'Make in India' and PLI schemes are yielding results in critical sectors.
• Discussions at international forums highlight the need for resilient supply chains post-pandemic and geopolitical disruptions.
• India is actively engaging in bilateral and multilateral initiatives to foster secure and diversified trade routes.
• Focus on developing robust logistics infrastructure and skilled workforce to capitalize on this opportunity.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: The PLI scheme aims to boost domestic manufacturing in various sectors.
India is part of the Supply Chain Resilience Initiative (SCRI) with Japan and Australia.
FDI is a major source of non-debt financial resources for India's economic development.
'Make in India' initiative was launched in 2014.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's strategic advantages and policy initiatives that are contributing to its emergence as a global supply chain hub.
Discuss the challenges India faces in fully realizing its potential in global manufacturing and supply chain diversification.
Evaluate the impact of schemes like 'Make in India' and PLI on enhancing domestic production and attracting foreign investment.
Examine the geopolitical implications of building resilient supply chains and India's role in shaping future trade architectures.
GS3.imageDescription: A world map with interconnected supply routes, highlighting India's central position.
GS3.trivia: The term 'supply chain' was first coined by Keith Oliver, a consultant at Booz Allen Hamilton, in 1982.

GS3.headline: India on Track to Exceed Renewable Energy Capacity Targets by 2030, Says Ministry Report
GS3.explainer:
• A recent report by the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy indicates that India is well-positioned to surpass its non-fossil fuel capacity targets ahead of 2030.
• Aggressive deployment of solar and wind energy projects, coupled with policy support, drives this growth.
• The country aims for 500 GW of non-fossil fuel-based energy capacity by 2030, a key commitment at COP26.
• Challenges include grid integration, land acquisition, and ensuring stable power supply from intermittent sources.
• Focus on green hydrogen and energy storage solutions to further accelerate the transition.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: India's target is 500 GW of non-fossil fuel-based energy capacity by 2030.
COP26 (Glasgow) saw India announce enhanced climate commitments.
The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) is the nodal ministry for renewable energy.
Solar and wind power are major components of India's renewable energy mix.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to India's rapid growth in renewable energy capacity and its implications for climate change mitigation.
Discuss the challenges associated with integrating large-scale renewable energy into the national grid and ensuring energy security.
Evaluate the potential of green hydrogen as a key enabler for India's energy transition and its economic implications.
Examine the policy and regulatory frameworks needed to further accelerate renewable energy deployment and investment in India.
GS3.imageDescription: A vast solar power plant or a wind farm against a blue sky, symbolizing renewable energy.
GS3.trivia: India is home to the world's largest single-location solar power project, the Bhadla Solar Park in Rajasthan.

GS3.headline: Animal Welfare Board Proposes Stricter Regulations for Pet Breeding and Sale
GS3.explainer:
• The Animal Welfare Board of India (AWBI) has proposed new, stringent regulations for the breeding, housing, and sale of companion animals.
• Aimed at curbing illegal pet trade, preventing cruelty, and ensuring ethical treatment of animals across the country.
• The draft rules mandate registration of breeders and pet shops, regular inspections, and adherence to minimum standards of care.
• Public feedback is being sought on the proposed regulations, with strong support from animal rights organizations.
• This move follows increasing concerns over puppy mills and inhumane conditions in unregulated pet markets.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
keyTerms: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960
prelimsPoints: The Animal Welfare Board of India (AWBI) is a statutory advisory body.
It was established under the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960.
AWBI advises the government on animal welfare laws and rules.

The 42nd Amendment Act added Article 48A (protection and improvement of environment and safeguarding of forests and wild life) and Article 51A(g) (fundamental duty to protect and improve the natural environment) to the Indian Constitution.
mainsPoints: Discuss the need for stricter regulations in the pet breeding and sale industry in India, citing instances of animal cruelty and exploitation.
Analyze the role of the Animal Welfare Board of India in promoting ethical treatment of animals and enforcing existing laws.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing and monitoring animal welfare regulations across a diverse and largely unregulated sector.
Examine the ethical dimensions of human-animal interaction and the societal responsibility towards animal welfare.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic depicting a happy pet animal alongside the AWBI logo and regulations.
GS3.trivia: The Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960, was enacted to prevent the infliction of unnecessary pain or suffering on animals.

GS3.headline: India Accelerates Chip Manufacturing Ambitions with New Fab Proposals
GS3.explainer:
• India's efforts to establish a robust semiconductor manufacturing ecosystem gain momentum with several new proposals for chip fabrication units.
• The 'India Semiconductor Mission' provides incentives and support for companies investing in local chip production.
• This strategic push aims to reduce reliance on global supply chains and enhance technological self-reliance.
• Challenges include securing advanced technology transfer, attracting skilled talent, and high initial investment costs.
• Government is focusing on both mature nodes and advanced packaging technologies to cater to diverse industry needs.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: The India Semiconductor Mission (ISM) was launched in 2021.
Semiconductors are essential components in almost all electronic devices.
The government offers incentives to attract investments in semiconductor manufacturing.
A 'fab' (fabrication plant) is a facility where integrated circuits (ICs) are manufactured.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of developing indigenous semiconductor manufacturing capabilities for India's economic and national security.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities associated with establishing a semiconductor ecosystem in India, including technological, financial, and human resource aspects.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government policies, such as the India Semiconductor Mission and PLI schemes, in attracting investment and fostering innovation.
Examine the potential geopolitical implications of India's entry into the global chip manufacturing landscape and its impact on supply chain resilience.
GS3.imageDescription: A microchip or a semiconductor wafer, symbolizing advanced technology.
GS3.trivia: The first integrated circuit (chip) was invented by Jack Kilby of Texas Instruments in 1958, for which he later shared the Nobel Prize in Physics.

GS3.headline: Namami Gange Phase-II: Focus Shifts to Tributary Rejuvenation and Sustainable River Basin Management
GS3.explainer:
• The second phase of the Namami Gange Programme intensifies efforts, extending its scope beyond the main stem of the Ganga to its tributaries.
• New projects sanctioned for pollution abatement, ecological restoration, and conservation of biodiversity in Yamuna, Ramganga, and other tributaries.
• Emphasis on sustainable river basin management, including afforestation, wetland conservation, and promotion of organic farming in catchment areas.
• Community participation and public awareness campaigns are being scaled up to ensure long-term success.
• Challenges include managing industrial and agricultural pollution, and ensuring inter-state coordination for tributary management.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Disaster and disaster management
prelimsPoints: Namami Gange Programme was launched in 2014.
It is an integrated conservation mission to abate pollution, conserve and rejuvenate the National River Ganga.
The National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG) is the implementing agency.
Ganga is India's national river.
mainsPoints: Analyze the objectives and achievements of the Namami Gange Programme Phase-I and the rationale behind extending it to tributaries in Phase-II.
Discuss the multi-faceted challenges in river basin management in India, particularly for large river systems like the Ganga and its tributaries.
Evaluate the role of various stakeholders, including government bodies, local communities, and NGOs, in river rejuvenation efforts.
Suggest innovative approaches and policy interventions for ensuring the sustainable health and ecological flow of India's rivers.
GS3.imageDescription: A clean stretch of the Ganga River with lush green banks, or a map highlighting Ganga and its tributaries.
GS3.trivia: The Ganga Action Plan, launched in 1986, was the first major initiative to clean the river Ganga.

sources: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship
